121.title: Aeneas and the Fall of Troy

vocabulary.definition: The events that will happen to a person in the future; fate
vocabulary.sentence: The gods had a plan for Aeneas's destiny.

vocabulary.definition: Deep sadness, especially caused by someone's death
vocabulary.sentence: He carried that grief all the way to Italy.

vocabulary.definition: Giving up something important for a greater good
vocabulary.sentence: Rome was built on sacrifice.

comprehension: Why did Aeneas leave Troy, according to Hector's ghost?
What difficult choice did Aeneas have to make in Carthage?
Who were Aeneas's parents, and what did this mean for his importance?
What does this story teach about the founding of Rome?
121.journal: Write about a time you had to choose between what you wanted and what was right. How did you make your decision?

informational.title: The Aeneid and Roman Literature
informational.part1: The story of Aeneas comes from a great Roman poem called the Aeneid, written by a poet named Virgil around 20 BC. Virgil spent over ten years writing this epic poem, which tells the entire journey of Aeneas from the fall of Troy to the founding of what would become Rome. The Roman Emperor Augustus specifically asked Virgil to write the Aeneid because Rome wanted a founding story as grand as the Greek Iliad and Odyssey. The Aeneid became one of the most important works in all of Western literature, studied by students for over two thousand years.
informational.part2: The Aeneid is different from Greek epics because it emphasizes duty and sacrifice over personal glory. While Greek heroes like Achilles fight for personal honor, Aeneas's journey is all about fulfilling his destiny to found a new civilization, even when it costs him personally. When Aeneas leaves Dido in Carthage, he is choosing his people's future over his own happiness. This reflects Roman values: the Romans believed that personal desires should be sacrificed for the good of the state. The phrase 'pious Aeneas' appears throughout the poem, describing his devotion to duty, family, and the gods—the three pillars of Roman virtue.

121.opinion: Do you think Aeneas made the right choice to leave Dido? Explain why duty to your people might sometimes mean giving up personal happiness.

grammar.skill: Recognizing complex sentences with dependent clauses
grammar.example: When Aeneas looked back at Carthage, he saw flames on the cliff above the harbor.

122.title: Cupid and Psyche

vocabulary.definition: A message from the gods, often mysterious or difficult to understand
vocabulary.sentence: Psyche's family received an oracle that was terrifying.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling resentful of someone else's good fortune
vocabulary.sentence: Whatever love they had for her was overcome by envy.

vocabulary.definition: A story where the characters and events represent ideas or lessons
vocabulary.sentence: The Romans told this story as an allegory about the soul and love.

comprehension: Why did Psyche's sisters convince her to look at her husband's face?
What were the four impossible tasks Venus gave to Psyche?
How did Psyche finally become immortal?
What do the names Psyche and Cupid represent in this allegory?
122.journal: Psyche had to complete difficult tasks to prove her love. Write about something difficult you've done to show someone you care about them.

informational.title: Allegory in Ancient Stories
informational.part1: An allegory is a story where the characters and events represent deeper meanings or moral lessons. The tale of Cupid and Psyche is one of the most famous allegories from ancient Rome. In this story, Psyche represents the human soul (her name literally means 'soul' in Greek), while Cupid represents divine love. The Romans used this story to teach that the soul must go through great difficulty and suffering before it can be fully united with love. Each of the impossible tasks Psyche faces represents challenges that the soul must overcome on its journey toward spiritual fulfillment.
informational.part2: This story was so popular in ancient Rome that it appeared in art, poetry, and philosophical discussions for centuries. The writer Apuleius included it in his novel 'The Golden Ass' around 160 AD, and it became one of the most retold stories in Western literature. Artists throughout history have painted scenes from the story, especially the moment when Psyche lights her lamp to see Cupid's face. The story teaches that trust broken can be rebuilt through perseverance, that love requires both partners to do difficult work, and that true union comes only after each person has proven themselves worthy through genuine suffering and growth.

122.opinion: Was Psyche wrong to look at Cupid's face? Explain whether you think trust or curiosity was more important in this situation.

grammar.example: The soul and love, the Romans believed, were always trying to find each other.

123.title: Juno's Wrath and Aeneas's Journey

vocabulary.definition: Causing great damage or suffering; disastrous
vocabulary.sentence: The storm was catastrophic.

vocabulary.definition: To last or continue despite difficulty
vocabulary.sentence: Made things endure.

vocabulary.definition: An agreement where each side gives up something to reach a solution
vocabulary.sentence: She tried a different approach: a compromise.

comprehension: Why did Juno hate Aeneas and want to stop him?
What deal did Juno make with Aeolus, the keeper of the winds?
What compromise did Juno eventually suggest to Venus?
How did Juno's attempts to stop Rome actually help shape what Rome became?
123.journal: Write about a time when something difficult turned out to make you stronger. How did that challenge change you for the better?

informational.title: The Roman Concept of Obstacles as Opportunities
informational.part1: The Romans had a unique philosophy about obstacles and challenges, which is clearly shown in the story of Juno's opposition to Aeneas. They believed that obstacles, when they cannot stop you, often improve you instead. This idea was central to Roman thinking about character development and success. In the story, Juno throws storm after storm at Aeneas, trying to prevent him from reaching Italy. But each challenge he faces makes him and his followers stronger, more determined, and more capable of building a great civilization. The Romans saw this pattern not just in mythology but in their own history.
informational.part2: This Roman philosophy is captured in several famous Latin phrases still used today. 'Per aspera ad astra' means 'through hardships to the stars'—showing that difficulty is the path to greatness. Another phrase, 'Adversity is the diamond dust Heaven polishes its jewels with,' reflects how challenges refine and improve us. The story of Juno and Aeneas taught Romans that their enemies, in trying to prevent Rome's rise, actually shaped Rome into something greater than it would have been with an easy path. This lesson influenced how Romans dealt with setbacks throughout their history: they looked for the benefit hidden in every difficulty.

123.opinion: Do you agree that obstacles can make people stronger? Give examples from your own life or from stories you know.

grammar.example: Because Juno made Aeneas's journey harder, the Trojans became stronger and Rome became a better city.

124.title: Jupiter, King of the Gods

vocabulary.definition: The power to give orders and make decisions
vocabulary.sentence: Jupiter held authority over all of them.

vocabulary.definition: Training that develops self-control and orderly conduct
vocabulary.sentence: Civilization required discipline.

vocabulary.definition: An advanced society with organized laws, culture, and government
vocabulary.sentence: War was Rome's way of extending the boundaries of civilization.

comprehension: How was the Roman Jupiter different from the Greek Zeus?
What three brothers divided the world, and what did each one rule?
What did Jupiter represent for the Roman state?
Why was Jupiter's temple on the Capitoline Hill so important?
124.journal: Jupiter represents order and law. Write about why you think rules and order are important in a community or family.

informational.title: The Temple of Jupiter and Roman Triumphs
informational.part1: The Temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus (Jupiter the Best and Greatest) stood on the Capitoline Hill, the highest of Rome's seven hills, overlooking the entire city. This temple was the most important religious building in Rome and the endpoint of every triumph—the grand parades celebrating Roman military victories. When a victorious general returned to Rome, he would lead a procession through the streets, displaying captured treasures and prisoners, while citizens cheered. The parade would climb the Capitoline Hill and end at Jupiter's temple, where the general would offer sacrifices to thank the god for victory.
informational.part2: The triumph ceremony was both a military celebration and a religious ritual, showing how closely connected Roman military success was to their chief god. The victorious general would dress like Jupiter himself, wearing a purple toga and a laurel crown, riding in a chariot. A slave would stand behind him whispering 'Remember, you are mortal' to prevent pride from overwhelming him. The triumph always ended at Jupiter's temple because Romans believed all victories ultimately belonged to the god who protected their state. The last known Roman triumph was celebrated in 403 AD, but the tradition had lasted for nearly 1,000 years.

124.opinion: Why do you think the Romans believed their victories belonged to Jupiter rather than just to their generals and soldiers?

grammar.example: Jupiter was not simply a storm god: He was the god of the state—of law and order and the proper way things should be done.

126.title: Mars, Father of Rome

vocabulary.definition: Exactness and accuracy in action or measurement
vocabulary.sentence: The Roman army was a precision instrument.

vocabulary.definition: The quality of not being able to be predicted or expected
vocabulary.sentence: The protector of flocks and fields from wolves and bandits and the hard unpredictability of nature.

vocabulary.definition: Continued effort despite difficulty
vocabulary.sentence: Trust broken can be rebuilt through perseverance.

comprehension: How was the Roman god Mars different from the Greek god Ares?
Why was March named after Mars?
What were Mars's two roles in Roman religion?
How does the story of Cincinnatus represent Mars's dual nature?
126.journal: Mars represents both defense and farming. Write about how someone can be both strong and gentle, depending on what the situation requires.

informational.title: The Roman Military and Citizen-Soldiers
informational.part1: The Roman army was unlike any military force before it. While most ancient armies were made up of nobles and their personal followers, Rome developed a system where ordinary citizens served as soldiers. Roman men were expected to be both farmers and warriors—working their fields in peacetime and fighting for Rome when needed. This created an army of disciplined, practical men who understood hard work and sacrifice. Mars, as the god of both war and agriculture, perfectly represented this ideal Roman citizen: someone who could plow a field or fight a battle with equal skill and dedication.
informational.part2: The legendary story of Cincinnatus, a Roman general called from his farm to save Rome, demonstrates this dual nature. In 458 BC, Rome was surrounded by enemies. The Senate appointed Cincinnatus as temporary dictator—a position of absolute power used only in emergencies. Messengers found him plowing his field. He accepted the position, led Rome to victory in just 16 days, then immediately gave up his power and returned to farming. George Washington, who gave up power after winning the Revolutionary War, was often compared to Cincinnatus. This ideal—service to country without seeking personal power—came directly from Roman values represented by Mars.

126.opinion: What do you think makes someone a good citizen in both peaceful times and times of trouble?

grammar.example: The ideal Roman citizen was a farmer who could pick up a sword when needed and put it down when the need was past.

127.title: Mercury, the Messenger

vocabulary.definition: Having or showing sharp judgment and cleverness
vocabulary.sentence: The same world of quick movement and shrewd judgment.

vocabulary.definition: A system of government with many complicated rules and procedures
vocabulary.sentence: He moved through divine bureaucracy with ease.

vocabulary.definition: A guide of souls to the underworld
vocabulary.sentence: This was called the role of psychopomp—guide of souls.

comprehension: What were Mercury's three main roles as a god?
Why did merchants pray to Mercury on the first of May?
What symbols represented Mercury?
How did Mercury help people who had died?
127.journal: Mercury was the god of communication and travelers. Write about a journey you've taken or a message you've delivered that was important.

informational.title: Roman Roads and the Movement of Ideas
informational.part1: The Roman road network was one of the greatest engineering achievements in history. At its peak, the Roman road system stretched over 250,000 miles, connecting Britain to the Middle East and North Africa to Germany. These roads were built to last—some Roman roads are still in use today, 2,000 years later. Romans built their roads with multiple layers: large stones for the foundation, smaller stones for drainage, gravel for smoothness, and fitted paving stones on top. The roads were slightly curved (called 'cambering') so rain would run off to the sides. Mercury, as the god of travelers, watched over every mile of these roads.
informational.part2: But the roads didn't just carry people—they carried ideas, culture, and unity. A merchant traveling from Spain to Syria would pass through dozens of different regions, all connected by Roman roads and protected by Roman law. News could travel from Britain to Rome in about a week using the imperial post system. This network of communication, trade, and travel helped hold the vast Roman Empire together. The phrase 'all roads lead to Rome' comes from how the road system radiated out from the capital like spokes on a wheel. Mercury's role as messenger god reflects how important communication and travel were to Roman civilization.

127.opinion: How do you think good communication and roads help hold a society together? Compare Roman roads to modern technology.

grammar.example: He was the messenger of the gods—the one who moved between all the worlds with equal ease.

128.title: Minerva and the Arts of Civilization

vocabulary.definition: The ability to do something successfully and efficiently
vocabulary.sentence: She was the divine patron of competence.

vocabulary.definition: Long and careful consideration or discussion
vocabulary.sentence: She also kept the gods' deliberations.

vocabulary.definition: An earlier event or action used as an example for similar situations
vocabulary.sentence: She could tell you the precedents.

comprehension: What kinds of skills and professions did Minerva protect?
How was Minerva born, and what did this represent?
What was special about the five-day festival Quinquatria?
Why was it important that Minerva had no spouse or children?
128.journal: Minerva was the goddess of wisdom and skill. Write about a skill you're learning and how practice is making you better at it.

informational.title: Education in Ancient Rome
informational.part1: Education was highly valued in ancient Rome, and students learned under Minerva's patronage. Roman children began school around age 7, learning to read and write using wax tablets and a stylus (a pointed tool for scratching letters). They memorized important texts, including the Twelve Tables of Roman law. Students sat on benches without backs, and classes were often held in shops or public spaces—there were no separate school buildings. Teachers (called ludi magistri) were sometimes slaves or freed slaves, and they maintained discipline with a rod. Education wasn't free; parents had to pay, so not all children could attend.
informational.part2: Older students who continued their education (usually only boys from wealthy families) studied the 'liberal arts': grammar, rhetoric (public speaking), logic, geometry, arithmetic, astronomy, and music. These seven subjects were considered essential for a free citizen (the word 'liberal' comes from 'liber' meaning 'free'). Students learned by memorization, recitation, and debate. The goal was to create well-rounded citizens who could speak eloquently in the Senate, understand law and mathematics, and appreciate culture. This Roman educational system influenced Western education for centuries—the 'liberal arts' taught at universities today come directly from this Roman tradition.

128.opinion: Minerva valued both physical skill (like fighting) and intellectual skill (like teaching). Why is it important to develop both your mind and your body?

grammar.example: The Roman army was a precision instrument, the most disciplined fighting force the ancient world had seen.

129.title: Neptune and the Sea

vocabulary.definition: Reluctant permission or tolerance
vocabulary.sentence: All of them sailed on Neptune's sufferance.

vocabulary.definition: A long period of abnormally low rainfall
vocabulary.sentence: In the midst of drought.

vocabulary.definition: A slightly curved surface, especially on a road
vocabulary.sentence: The roads were slightly curved (called 'cambering').

comprehension: Why did people pray to Neptune only when they needed him?
What competition did Neptune lose to Minerva, and how did he react?
What was special about the festival Neptunalia?
Why was Neptune connected to both the sea and horses?
129.journal: Neptune controlled something powerful and unpredictable—the sea. Write about a force of nature that seems powerful to you and how people deal with it.

informational.title: Roman Engineering and Water Management
informational.part1: While Neptune ruled the natural water—the seas, rivers, and lakes—the Romans became masters of controlling water through engineering. They built massive aqueducts to bring fresh water from mountain springs to their cities. The Aqua Claudia aqueduct, completed in 52 AD, carried water 45 miles to Rome, using gravity to maintain a constant flow. Some sections were underground tunnels, while others featured soaring stone arches up to 100 feet high. Rome had 11 major aqueducts supplying about 200 gallons of water per person per day—more than most modern cities provide. The water powered public fountains, baths, toilets, and even decorative features.
informational.part2: Romans also built extensive harbor works to protect ships from Neptune's storms. The harbor at Ostia, Rome's main port, featured massive breakwaters made from a special concrete that could set underwater (a technology lost for centuries after Rome's fall). They used a volcanic ash called pozzolana mixed with lime to create this waterproof concrete. This engineering prowess didn't replace religion—Romans still prayed to Neptune for safe voyages—but it showed their belief that human skill and divine favor should work together. The Romans honored Neptune while also building walls against his storms.

129.opinion: The Romans believed in praying to gods while also using engineering to solve problems. How do you balance trusting in help from others with working hard yourself?

grammar.example: It asked Neptune for fair winds and calm water, and it ended with a reminder to the god of something the god certainly knew: we are small, and the sea is large, and we are trusting you.

131.title: Proserpina and the Pomegranate

vocabulary.definition: The act of taking someone away by force
vocabulary.sentence: The broad outline was the same as the Greek story—abduction.

vocabulary.definition: An agreement where each side gives up something
vocabulary.sentence: Mercury explained the compromise.

vocabulary.definition: Changed in form, appearance, or character
vocabulary.sentence: She was transformed by it.

comprehension: What happened to the grain when Proserpina was taken?
What was the rule about eating food in the underworld?
What compromise allowed Proserpina to spend part of each year on earth?
How did Proserpina change during her time in the underworld?
131.journal: The story says suffering can transform you into something more than you were. Write about a difficult experience that changed you or made you stronger.

informational.title: The Grain Supply and Roman Power
informational.part1: Grain was the foundation of Roman power. Most Romans ate bread as their main food source, consuming about two pounds of bread per day. Rome's population at its peak was over one million people, requiring thousands of tons of grain every month. Most of this grain came from Egypt, North Africa, and Sicily—distant provinces connected to Rome by sea. The grain fleet was so important that it operated under imperial protection. Ceres, the goddess of grain, represented something essential to Roman survival: the food supply that kept the city alive and prevented riots.
informational.part2: The Roman government provided free grain to about 200,000 citizens through a program called the 'grain dole' (annona). This wasn't charity—it was politics. Leaders understood that hungry citizens were dangerous citizens. The phrase 'bread and circuses' comes from the Roman practice of keeping people happy with free grain (bread) and entertainment (circuses). When grain shipments were delayed or the harvest failed, riots could erupt in the streets. The festival of Cerealia honored Ceres every spring, praying for good harvests and celebrating Proserpina's return. This agricultural festival was as important to Roman security as their military might.

131.opinion: Why do you think food security (making sure everyone has enough to eat) is important for a society's stability?

grammar.skill: Using semicolons to connect related ideas
grammar.example: She did not merely endure the underworld—she governed it beside Pluto; she received the prayers of the dying; she judged with her husband.

132.title: Romulus and Remus

vocabulary.definition: A person forced to live away from their home or country
vocabulary.sentence: Adventurers came, and runaway slaves, and exiles.

vocabulary.definition: The practice of interpreting omens from birds to predict the future
vocabulary.sentence: The augury—the watching of birds—gave twelve vultures to Romulus.

vocabulary.definition: The act of making someone a god
vocabulary.sentence: The official story was deification.

comprehension: How were Romulus and Remus saved as babies?
What disagreement led to Remus's death?
How did Romulus populate his new city?
What lesson did the Romans learn from how their city was founded?
132.journal: The she-wolf showed mercy to the abandoned babies. Write about a time when someone showed unexpected kindness to you or to someone else.

informational.title: The Founding Date of Rome
informational.part1: According to Roman tradition, Romulus founded Rome on April 21, 753 BC. The Romans celebrated this date every year as the 'birthday of Rome' (Natalis Urbis). But how did they calculate this exact date? Ancient Roman scholars, especially a historian named Marcus Terentius Varro, worked backward from known dates in Roman history, counting the reigns of kings and the years of the Republic to arrive at this founding date. Whether this date is historically accurate is debatable—archaeology suggests people lived on Rome's hills long before 753 BC—but Romans treated it as their official beginning.
informational.part2: The founding date became part of how Romans counted years. While we say '2026 AD,' Romans would say something like '2,779 AUC'—ab urbe condita, meaning 'from the founding of the city.' This way of counting years reminded Romans of their connection to their legendary beginning. Modern scholars debate when the historical city of Rome actually emerged as a unified settlement, but April 21, 753 BC remains the traditional founding date. Rome's birthday festival included horse races, theatrical performances, and ceremonies honoring the she-wolf and the city's divine origins.

132.opinion: The Romans didn't hide the difficult parts of their founding story. Why is it important to be honest about history, even when it's not flattering?

grammar.example: She nursed them, bringing them food, curled around them.

133.title: The Founding of Rome

vocabulary.definition: Removed someone from power by force
vocabulary.sentence: His brother Amulius overthrew Numitor.

vocabulary.definition: A safe place; shelter from danger
vocabulary.sentence: He opened it as a refuge.

vocabulary.definition: Showing disrespect or scorn
vocabulary.sentence: A contemptuous gesture.

comprehension: Why did Amulius force Rhea Silvia to become a Vestal Virgin?
How did the she-wolf come to care for the twin babies?
What method did Romulus and Remus use to decide where to build their city?
How did the Sabine women prevent a battle between their fathers and husbands?
133.journal: The Sabine women stood between two armies to make peace. Write about a time when someone helped make peace between people who were fighting.

informational.title: The Seven Hills of Rome
informational.part1: Rome was built on seven hills: the Palatine, Aventine, Capitoline, Quirinal, Viminal, Esquiline, and Caelian. Each hill had its own character and importance in Roman history. The Palatine Hill was where Romulus built the original city, and it later became the location of the emperor's palace (the word 'palace' comes from 'Palatine'). The Capitoline Hill held the great temple of Jupiter. The Aventine Hill was associated with the plebeians (common people). The seven hills weren't particularly tall—the highest is only about 150 feet above the Tiber River—but they provided natural defensive positions.
informational.part2: The hills shaped how Rome grew and functioned. Valleys between the hills became gathering places: the Roman Forum was in the valley between the Capitoline and Palatine hills. As Rome expanded, the city eventually spread beyond the seven hills, but Romans always remembered that their city had begun on these hilltops above the Tiber. The boundary of the seven hills marked the pomerium—the sacred boundary of the original city, which no one could cross with weapons and where certain religious rules applied. This sacred boundary, first marked by Romulus with his plow, remained important in Roman law and religion for centuries.

133.opinion: Romulus opened his city to anyone who needed a home. What are the benefits and challenges of welcoming people from many different backgrounds?

grammar.example: Romulus wept for what he had done, buried Remus with great honor, and then went back to building.

134.title: The Story of Lucretia

vocabulary.definition: Having high moral standards
vocabulary.sentence: They decided to see what each woman was actually doing—whose wife was most virtuous.

vocabulary.definition: Friendly and generous reception of guests
vocabulary.sentence: She received him with proper Roman hospitality.

vocabulary.definition: An earlier example that is used as a guide
vocabulary.sentence: No unchaste woman would ever use Lucretia as her precedent.

comprehension: Why was Lucretia considered the most virtuous woman in Rome?
What terrible threat did Sextus Tarquinius use against Lucretia?
How did the Roman people respond when they learned what happened to Lucretia?
What government system replaced the kings after this event?
134.journal: Lucretia's story led to a major change in how Rome was governed. Write about how one person's courage can inspire others to make important changes.

informational.title: The Roman Republic: A New Form of Government
informational.part1: After the overthrow of the kings in 509 BC, Rome created a completely new form of government: the Republic. Instead of one king holding power for life, Rome would be governed by two consuls elected each year by the citizen assembly. The consuls had equal power and could veto each other's decisions, preventing either one from becoming too powerful. After their year in office, they returned to being private citizens, ensuring that no one held power indefinitely. This system was designed specifically to prevent the kind of tyranny that the Tarquin kings had represented.
informational.part2: The word 'republic' comes from Latin 'res publica' meaning 'public thing' or 'commonwealth'—the idea that government belonged to the people, not to a king. The Roman Republic also included a Senate (a council of elder statesmen who advised the consuls), popular assemblies where citizens voted on laws, and various other officials elected for specific duties. This system of checks and balances, where different parts of government limited each other's power, influenced later democratic governments. The American Founding Fathers studied the Roman Republic carefully when designing the U.S. government, borrowing ideas like the Senate, the veto power, and the concept of separating governmental powers.

134.opinion: Why do you think the Romans believed it was better to have leaders elected for short terms rather than kings who ruled for life?

grammar.skill: Recognizing the purpose of quotation marks for emphasis
grammar.example: The word 'republic' comes from Latin 'res publica' meaning 'public thing'.

136.title: The Twelve Labors: The Roman Hercules

vocabulary.definition: Continuing to do something despite difficulty
vocabulary.sentence: The grinding persistence of a man doing twelve impossible things.

vocabulary.definition: Calmness and self-control
vocabulary.sentence: The Romans loved this—not just the cleverness of it but the composure.

vocabulary.definition: Cruel and oppressive government or rule
vocabulary.sentence: Force without love was tyranny.

comprehension: What was the 'Choice of Hercules' story, and what did it teach?
How did Hercules clean the Augean Stables?
How did Hercules trick Atlas into taking back the sky?
Why did merchants and soldiers pray to Hercules?
136.journal: Hercules had to choose between an easy life and a hard but meaningful one. Write about a time you chose to do something difficult because it was the right thing to do.

informational.title: The Choice of Hercules in Western Art and Philosophy
informational.part1: The story of Hercules choosing between Virtue and Pleasure became one of the most painted and discussed moral lessons in Western civilization. The ancient philosopher Prodicus first told this version of Hercules's story in the 5th century BC, and it was retold by Roman writers and adopted by Christian teachers in the Middle Ages. Renaissance artists painted hundreds of versions showing young Hercules standing at a crossroads between two women: Virtue (often shown as plain and serious, pointing up a rocky mountain path) and Pleasure (shown as attractive and comfortable, pointing to an easy downhill path).
informational.part2: This moral allegory influenced Western thinking about ethics for over 2,000 years. Parents used it to teach children that the worthwhile path in life requires effort and discipline. Schools used it to encourage students to study hard rather than take shortcuts. The phrase 'Herculean effort' entered our language from this tradition, meaning work that should be impossible but is completed through determination. The basic lesson—that meaningful achievement requires choosing difficulty over ease—remains relevant. Every time someone studies for a test instead of playing games, or practices a skill instead of watching TV, they're making the Choice of Hercules.

136.opinion: Do you agree that the difficult path is more worthwhile than the easy path? Give reasons for your answer.

grammar.example: The story of Hercules choosing between Virtue and Pleasure became one of the most-painted moral lessons.

137.title: The Twelve Tables and Roman Law

vocabulary.definition: An official who administers the law
vocabulary.sentence: They were the magistrates who applied the law.

vocabulary.definition: A group of people sent to represent others
vocabulary.sentence: The Romans sent a delegation to Greece.

vocabulary.definition: Highly developed and complex
vocabulary.sentence: Rome developed one of the most sophisticated legal systems.

comprehension: Why did the plebeians demand that the law be written down?
What did the plebeians do to force the patricians to negotiate?
What were the Twelve Tables, and where were they displayed?
How do the Twelve Tables influence modern law?
comprehension.journal: The story teaches that written laws help ensure fairness. Write about why you think it's important that everyone knows what the rules are.

informational.title: From Roman Law to Modern Legal Systems
informational.part1: Roman law is the foundation of most legal systems in the modern world. When Rome fell in the 5th century AD, its legal principles survived in the Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire. The Byzantine Emperor Justinian ordered all Roman law collected and organized into the 'Corpus Juris Civilis' (Body of Civil Law) in 529-534 AD. This massive work preserved centuries of Roman legal thinking. When universities began forming in medieval Europe (around 1100 AD), law students studied this Roman legal code. The principles spread throughout Europe and eventually to European colonies worldwide.
informational.part2: Many fundamental legal concepts we take for granted come from Rome: the presumption of innocence (innocent until proven guilty), the right to face your accuser, the use of lawyers and evidence, the distinction between civil and criminal law, and the idea that laws should be written clearly and applied consistently. The American legal system, while based on English common law, incorporated many Roman principles. Latin legal phrases are still used in courtrooms today: 'habeas corpus' (produce the body), 'subpoena' (under penalty), 'per se' (by itself). About 60% of modern countries use 'civil law' systems directly descended from Roman law, while most others still show Roman influence.

comprehension.opinion: The Twelve Tables made law visible to everyone. How do you think technology today (like the internet) helps people understand their rights and laws?

grammar.example: Around 450 BC (Before Christ), the Romans sent a delegation to Greece to study the laws of Solon.

138.title: Venus, Mother of Rome

vocabulary.definition: Relating to family history and descent
vocabulary.sentence: Not metaphorically, but in the serious genealogical way.

vocabulary.definition: Lacking serious purpose; not important
vocabulary.sentence: Beauty was not frivolous.

vocabulary.definition: Controlling someone or something in a clever or dishonest way
vocabulary.sentence: She was not above manipulation in service of the people she loved.

comprehension: How was Venus connected to the city of Rome?
What temple did Julius Caesar build for Venus, and why?
Why did the Romans choose both Mars and Venus as divine ancestors?
What month was named for Venus, and why?
138.journal: Venus was the goddess of beauty and love. Write about something beautiful you've created or seen, and why beauty matters to people.

informational.title: Beauty and Power in Roman Art and Architecture
informational.part1: The Romans were master builders who combined engineering skill with artistic beauty. They didn't just build roads—they built them with elegant mile markers and arches. They didn't just build aqueducts—they built them with soaring arches that are still photographed today. Roman temples featured carefully proportioned columns, elaborate carvings, and brilliant white marble that gleamed in the sun. Public buildings in the Forum featured colorful frescoes, intricate mosaics, and sculptures of gods and heroes. Even ordinary apartment buildings had decorative details. This attention to beauty reflected their belief that Venus, their divine mother, demanded it.
informational.part2: Roman art and architecture influenced Western civilization for two thousand years. Renaissance artists studied Roman buildings and sculptures. American government buildings (like the Capitol and Supreme Court) copied Roman temple designs with columns and triangular pediments. The Romans believed that public spaces should inspire citizens through beauty—that living in a beautiful city made people better. They spent enormous resources making their empire not just functional but magnificent. This idea—that beauty serves a purpose, that it uplifts and civilizes—came partly from their belief that Venus watched over them and expected them to honor her through beautiful creation.

138.opinion: Do you think beauty is important, or are practical things (that work well) more important? Can something be both beautiful and practical?

grammar.example: Making things well, making things to last, making things that served their purpose—this was sacred work.

139.title: Vulcan, the Divine Craftsman

vocabulary.definition: Involving or causing sudden great damage
vocabulary.sentence: The storm was catastrophic.

vocabulary.definition: Changed completely in form or nature
vocabulary.sentence: He transformed raw material into something better.

vocabulary.definition: Basic systems and structures needed for a society to function
vocabulary.sentence: The whole physical infrastructure of civilization.

comprehension: What happened to Vulcan that caused him to walk with a limp?
What were some of the famous things Vulcan made for the gods?
Why did the Romans hold Vulcan's festival in late August?
What is the relationship between Vulcan and things we still name after him today?
139.journal: Vulcan turned his suffering into skill and became the greatest craftsman. Write about how you've learned from a difficulty or used a challenge to become better at something.

informational.title: Roman Technology and Practical Innovation
informational.part1: The Romans were extraordinary engineers and inventors who transformed raw materials into lasting works, just as Vulcan did. They perfected concrete construction, creating a mixture that could set underwater and last for millennia. They built the Colosseum (seating 50,000+ people) with concrete, arches, and a sophisticated system of ramps and pulleys for moving animals and scenery. They created central heating (hypocaust system) that sent hot air under floors and through walls. They built public bathhouses with hot, warm, and cold pools, using aqueduct water and sophisticated plumbing. They made glass windows, created surgical instruments still recognizable today, and built cranes that could lift multi-ton stones.
informational.part2: Many Roman technologies were lost after Rome fell and had to be reinvented centuries later. Their concrete formula was lost until modern times (though we've now figured out it included volcanic ash). Their advanced medical knowledge disappeared. But Romans also left practical innovations that never went away: the 24-hour day, the Julian calendar (basis for our modern calendar), the book format (instead of scrolls), public libraries, and standardized measurements. The Romans honored Vulcan, the god of useful making, by building things designed to last and to serve practical purposes. Many Roman roads, aqueducts, and buildings still stand 2,000 years later—testimony to their dedication to making things well.

139.opinion: Vulcan shows that the things we make can outlast us. What do you think people today should build or create that will still be valuable 100 years from now?

grammar.example: He took iron and made weapons from it. He took gold and made jewelry from it. He took bronze and made armor from it.
